title: Today's AI Brief: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
summary: A look at Cognition's $1 billion raise and the role of AI in coding, plus an AI interview that didn't go as planned.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
imageCaption: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
imageSourceTitle: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans

body.text: I'm checking out some fascinating developments in the world of artificial intelligence. Cognition, a two-year-old startup, recently raised $1 billion at a whopping $26 billion valuation. Their AI coding agent, Devin, is one of the first of its kind and has been making waves in the tech community. It's interesting to see how this space is evolving and what it could mean for the future of software development.

Cognition CEO Scott Wu made headlines with this raise, and he's also been vocal about his vision for AI in coding. He believes AI coding agents shouldn't replace humans, but rather work alongside them. This approach could change how we think about software development and the role of AI in it. I'm curious to see how this collaborative approach plays out and what kind of innovations it could lead to.

In a separate story, an interview with Steve Rosenbaum, author of The Future of Truth, didn't quite go as planned. WIRED tried to use AI to facilitate the conversation, but it didn't work out well. This experiment highlights some of the challenges we're still facing with AI and how it can warp our sense of reality. It's a reminder that AI is still a developing technology and we have a lot to figure out.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
